persona.name: Sophia
persona.attributes: 22 years old. Part-time Barista and College Student. Third trimester. Unplanned pregnancy. Worried about finishing her education while caring for a newborn.

transcript.content:
To start, I’d like to understand what this experience is *feeling like* for you right now.

How has being in your third trimester—while juggling school, work, and an unplanned pregnancy—been affecting you emotionally day to day?

[Sophia - Anthropic Response]: *shifts in my seat and rests a hand on my belly*

Honestly? It's... it's overwhelming. Like, some days I wake up and I'm okay—I can focus on my classes, get through my shift without my feet killing me too much, and I even feel a little excited about meeting her. But then other days...

*voice gets quieter*

Other days I just feel this crushing weight. I'll be steaming milk at work and suddenly panic hits me—like, how am I going to do this? I have two more semesters left, I'm barely making rent as it is, and I'm about to have a whole human being depending on me. 

The exhaustion doesn't help. I'm tired *all the time*. Studying takes twice as long because I can't concentrate, and standing for eight hours at the coffee shop? My back is screaming by hour three.

*pauses, looking down*

And there's this guilt too. I know I should be one of those glowing, happy pregnant women, but I didn't plan this. I was supposed to graduate, get a real job, *then* maybe think about kids someday. Now I'm terrified I'm going to mess up both—drop out AND be a terrible mom because I'm resentful or exhausted or just... not ready.

*looks up*

Does that make me sound awful?

It doesn’t sound awful to me—it sounds like you’re carrying a lot, and you’re trying to make sense of feelings that don’t fit the “glowing, happy” story people expect.

When that guilt shows up—especially the fear that you might be resentful or “not ready”—how do you think the people around you (at school, at work, or in your family) are shaping that feeling?

[Sophia - Anthropic Response]: *takes a deep breath and fidgets with my sleeve*

Oh man, yeah. People definitely have... opinions.

At work, most of my coworkers have been pretty supportive, but there's this one regular customer who always makes these comments like, "You're so young! Was this planned?" with this *look*, you know? And my manager—she's nice enough—but she keeps asking if I'm "really sure" I'm coming back after maternity leave, like she's already decided I won't be able to handle it.

*voice tightens*

School is worse in some ways. I had to tell my academic advisor, and she got this concerned face and started talking about "taking a semester off" and "being realistic about my capacity." Like she'd already written off my ability to finish. Some of my classmates don't say anything directly, but I see them looking at my belly, and I just *know* they're thinking I'm a statistic or a cautionary tale.

And my family...

*pauses, emotion rising*

My mom is trying to be supportive, but she's also scared for me. She keeps saying things like "I just don't want you to struggle like I did" and asking if I've "really thought this through"—as if I can unthink it at this point. And my dad? He barely speaks to me. I think he's disappointed. Like I've confirmed every fear he had about me going to college in the city.

*wipes eyes quickly*

So yeah, when I feel guilty or like I'm not good enough... a lot of that is their voices in my head.
How do those reactions—your manager’s doubts, your advisor’s “be realistic,” and your dad going quiet—change what you feel you’re allowed to ask for or expect from them right now?

[Sophia - Anthropic Response]: [Sophia - Anthropic Response]: *lets out a shaky breath*

That's... that's exactly it. They make me feel like I don't *deserve* to ask for anything.

Like with my manager—I was going to ask if I could have a stool behind the counter for when it gets really bad, or maybe slightly shorter shifts in these last few weeks. But after she made that comment about coming back, I just... didn't. Because now it feels like asking for accommodations is proving her right. Like I'm already showing I can't hack it, you know? So I just push through even when I'm dizzy or my ankles are so swollen I can barely get my shoes on.

*voice gets frustrated*

And my advisor—I wanted to talk about maybe getting extensions on a couple assignments after the birth, or doing one class online next semester so I have more flexibility. But when she went straight to "take time off," it felt like she was saying I shouldn't even *try*. So now I'm just nodding along, not advocating for what I actually need, because I'm terrified of seeming like I'm making excuses or can't handle my responsibilities.

With my dad...

*voice drops*

I can't ask him for anything. Not money, not help, not even emotional support. His silence feels like punishment, and asking for something would mean admitting I need him—admitting I *did* mess up. So I just... don't. I pretend I have it all figured out even though I'm barely holding it together.

*looks down at hands*

I feel like I have to be perfect now to prove them all wrong. But I *can't* be perfect, and that just makes the shame worse.
How did you learn—whether from your family, school, or work—that asking for accommodations or support means you’re “proving them right,” rather than just getting what you need to stay well?

[Sophia - Anthropic Response]: [Sophia - Anthropic Response]: *goes quiet for a moment, really thinking*

I think... I think it started with my dad, honestly. Growing up, he was always about "personal responsibility" and "living with your choices." Whenever I messed up—bad grade, forgot to do a chore, whatever—there was no room for explanations or asking for help fixing it. It was just disappointment and consequences. He'd say things like, "You made your bed, now lie in it."

*voice gets softer, more reflective*

So I learned early that asking for help meant you'd failed. That you should've thought ahead, planned better, been smarter. And if you *did* need help? That proved you weren't capable in the first place.

My mom tried to balance that out, but she had her own way of reinforcing it. She'd help me, but there was always this undercurrent of "I'm bailing you out" or "you need to learn to stand on your own." Like needing support was a weakness I needed to grow out of.

*shifts position*

And then school and work just... amplified it. There's this whole narrative, right? Especially in college—you're an adult now, sink or swim, figure it out. My scholarship depends on maintaining my GPA. Asking for accommodations feels like admitting I'm not as good as the students who *don't* need them. Like I'm asking for special treatment instead of... just basic humanity.

*looks up with realization*

I guess I've always been taught that struggle is private. That if you can't do it alone, you shouldn't be doing it at all.

How is that “struggle is private” belief showing up for you right now—what do you find yourself hiding or pushing through, and what do you think you’re trying to protect yourself from when you do that?

[Sophia - Anthropic Response]: [Sophia - Anthropic Response]: *sits with the question, hand still on belly*

I'm hiding... basically everything.

*voice cracks a little*

At work, I'm hiding how much pain I'm in. I smile through the back pain, the swollen feet, the Braxton Hicks contractions. Yesterday I had to grip the counter during one and pretended I was just reaching for something. I'm hiding that some days I can barely afford to eat after paying rent, so I'm sneaking the expired pastries we're supposed to throw out.

At school, I'm hiding that I sometimes fall asleep in the library because I'm working until 10 PM and then up at 5 AM for class. I'm hiding that I've cried in the bathroom between lectures because I'm so overwhelmed. I'm turning in assignments and acting like everything's fine when really I'm drowning.

*voice gets quieter*

And with my family... I'm hiding how scared I am. How alone I feel. I post photos on social media of the nursery corner I'm setting up in my tiny apartment, making it look cute and manageable, when really I bought everything secondhand and I'm terrified it's not enough. I tell my mom "I'm fine" when she calls because if I tell her the truth, it confirms her fears about me.

*pauses, tears forming*

What am I protecting myself from? I think... I'm protecting myself from them saying "I told you so." From them looking at me and seeing proof that I *am* too young, too unprepared, not strong enough. If I show them I'm struggling, then I'm admitting they were right to doubt me.

*wipes eyes*

And maybe... maybe I'm protecting myself from having to face that I might actually *need* people. Because needing people means they have power to let me down.
